taskVersion.name: epu_q2_long
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_long")
def epu_q2_long(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, long_pre, long_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
The surprise resignation of Mike Simpson, executive director of the troubled Arts Center of North Texas project, might signify a fresh funding crisis and perhaps the death knell for the planned project in Allen. Simpson's resignation was announced in a news release by the center's board of directors on Friday and is effective immediately. The center's board had recommended a month ago that the overall budget be reduced. "So the writing was on the wall that this would put pressure on our personnel costs in a fairly quick manner," said Bobby Baggett, chairman of the board of directors. Simpson agreed that the problem was money. Six months into the current fiscal year, he said, the project was still far from the fundraising goals he and the board had set. "We have a lot of gtod things happening, but there was a concern that those options were going to take six, nine, maybe 12 months more to get done," Simpson said. "The board was concerned about continuing to fund the operations out of donations with the long time period it was going to take to find solutions. "In the interest of the project and helping it to move forward, that's why I'm doing what I'm doing now," he said. Simpson's salary is the project's largest administrative expense. The news ttok leaders of the cities involved in the project and art patrons by surprise, but Baggett saideconomicrealities required changes. When Frisco voters decided last May that the city should withdraw its commitment for $16.4 million in bo

[...TRUNCATED...]

ackage totaling] $192 million in bonds. "The only part that wasn't funded was the arts center." Simpson was under frequent attack during the Frisco election campaign, "which was unfortunate, because he was quite ill during this time," Nunn said. "But that didn't seem to bother anyone who was attacking him." And despite Simpson's disappointment in the way things have gone, his enthusiasm for the art center hasn't flagged. "I am still going to do everything I can in my own way to make it," he said. "If they restructure and they need volunteers to do anything, I'm willing to do anything and everything to make it happen. It's a great project." He has no immediate career plans, though. "I've just spent three years of my life working on this project as hard as any project I have ever been involved with," he said. The arts center board will meet next week to decide on its next steps. "Whether the ultimate decision is to put the project on hold or spin it off to an independent nonprofit or something else, my sense is the best option will involve a long-term approach," Baggett said. "We really don't have any time pressures concerning the arts center at this time. "The next steps are really a joint decision of our owner cities," he said. "I can't speak for the board, but my sense from almost every leader in the community I talk to is they do not want to see the project shut down." myoung@dallasnews.com; jweiss@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): MIKE SIMPSON
TARGET ARTICLE END
